---
title: "What's Vulnerable, Part Two: Risk vs. Behavioral, and Overlapping Inference"
date: "2026-07-20"
---

# What's Vulnerable: The Last Two Threats {.title}

::: notes
Welcome back to the reader's guide for Jegadeesh and Titman, part ten of eleven. In part nine we covered transaction costs, data-snooping, and seasonality. Now we finish the vulnerabilities with the last two — the genuinely unresolved risk-versus-behavioral debate, and the overlapping-portfolio inference problem one more time. These two are different in kind: one is about interpretation, the other about the arithmetic of the standard errors.
:::

## Risk-Based vs. Behavioral: Unresolved

- Grant the profits are real and net-positive — but **why**?
- **Behavioral:** investors underreact, then later overreact
- **Risk-based:** momentum loads on priced, time-varying risk
- The two thousand nine **crash** is the crash-risk exhibit
- This paper predates the evidence; the interpretation is **open**

::: notes
The fourth threat is interpretive: the risk-based versus behavioral debate, and it is genuinely unresolved. Even granting the profits are real and net-positive, why do they exist? The behavioral camp says investors underreact to news — prices drift toward fundamentals slowly — and later overreact, producing the eventual long-run reversal. The risk-based camp says momentum loads on some priced, time-varying macro risk, and they point to the spectacular two thousand nine momentum crash, when beaten-down losers rocketed in the recovery and the short leg blew up, as evidence that momentum carries crash risk you are compensated for bearing. This paper cannot adjudicate the debate; it predates the evidence on both sides. A careful reader reports the anomaly and explicitly labels the interpretation as open, rather than picking a winner the data does not yet support.
:::

## Overlapping-Portfolio Inference, Again

- If $t$-stats ignore the $K-1$ induced autocorrelation, they're overstated
- Won't erase a $t$ of six; can sink a marginal one
- First thing to recompute in replication
- **Newey-West** $t$-stats with lag at least $K-1$

::: notes
The fifth threat brings us back to Week Two one more time: overlapping-portfolio inference. If the paper's t-statistics do not correct for the K-minus-one induced autocorrelation, they are overstated. This does not erase a t of, say, six — a result that strong survives the correction comfortably — but it can sink a marginal one near the threshold of significance. So the first thing to recompute in any replication is the Newey-West t-statistics with lag length at least K minus one. Put the naive and corrected standard errors side by side and watch how much the inference moves. For the strongest cells, very little; for the weakest, possibly the difference between significant and not. That side-by-side is the single most informative diagnostic you can produce from this data.
:::

## The Honest Reader's Posture

- The anomaly is **real** and replicable — concede that
- Net-of-cost survival is **contested**, leg-dependent
- The mechanism is **open**: risk or behavior
- Report the effect; do not oversell the interpretation

::: notes
Step back and adopt the honest reader's posture. The momentum anomaly is real and replicable — concede that plainly; it is one of the most robust patterns in all of asset pricing. But its survival net of realistic costs is contested and depends on which leg you weight, especially the expensive small-cap short side. And the mechanism — whether it is a missing risk factor or a behavioral underreaction-then-overreaction story — is genuinely open. The right stance is to report the effect cleanly, quantify the costs, and refuse to oversell the interpretation. You take a side only with evidence you can show, and you flag honestly where the evidence runs out. That posture is what separates a finding from a sales pitch.
:::

## Key Takeaway {.title}

::: callout-tip
## Key takeaway
Two final threats: the **risk-versus-behavioral** mechanism is unresolved — underreaction-then-overreaction versus compensated crash risk, with two thousand nine as the exhibit — and **overlapping inference** demands Newey-West $t$-stats at lag $\geq K-1$. Report the effect; flag the open questions. Next: replication.
:::

::: notes
To summarize part ten: the last two vulnerabilities. The risk-versus-behavioral debate is genuinely unresolved — behavioral underreaction and later overreaction on one side, compensated time-varying crash risk on the other, with the two thousand nine momentum crash as the central exhibit — and this paper predates the deciding evidence, so the interpretation stays open. And overlapping-portfolio inference demands Newey-West t-statistics at lag at least K minus one, which can sink a marginal result though not a strong one. The honest posture is to report the real anomaly while flagging the open cost and mechanism questions. In part eleven we close with the three replication exercises and the referee's questions.
:::
